data.title: Georgia Power restores power to 230,000+ customers following Winter Storm Cora
data.description:
Georgia Power restores power to 230,000+ customers following Winter Storm Cora

Georgia Power restores power to 230,000+ customers following Winter Storm Cora

PR Newswire

ATLANTA, Jan. 11, 2025

 Crews navigating icy roads, wind and continuing winter weather in restoration efforts;

Company expects to restore power to nearly all impacted customers Saturday 

ATLANTA, Jan. 11, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- Following Winter Storm Cora, which brought heavy snow, freezing rain and ice to North Georgia, Georgia Power has restored power to more than 230,000 customers since the start of the storm. As of Saturday morning, less than 60,000 customers remain impacted. Crews are in the field now and the company expects that nearly all impacted customers who can receive power, will be reconnected today. The company's rapid response to the storm has been possible through the implementation of new "smart grid" technologies and the quick work of pre-positioned teams who were ready to respond as soon as conditions were safe to do so.

Many areas experienced additional damage overnight from falling trees and limbs with the hardest hit areas including parts of Alpharetta, Jonesboro and Atlanta. As crews work today, they are starting with damage assessment, which is an essential part of efficient restoration. These damage assessment teams are followed by repair crews depending on the amount of damage and access to the area. As weather conditions improve, restoration efforts can accelerate, however, with temperatures expected to barely rise above freezing in many areas Saturday, Georgia Power notes that challenging road conditions and the potential for continued falling ice, limbs and trees may slow progress or cause additional damage.  

Georgia Power appreciates the importance of accurate information for customers as they make plans for their families. The company continues to post the latest information, including estimated restoration times (ERTs) on its Outage Map as available, based on the latest information from field crews. Visit the online Storm Center to access the Outage Map and additional information.

Safety Tips for Customers and Crews

Georgia Power reminds customers that dangerous conditions exist following a storm, and to always heed the advice of local, state, and federal emergency authorities in storm-affected areas.

  • Watch for Georgia Power crews working across the state. If you must be on the roads, please move over one lane for utility vehicles stopped on the side of the road – it's the law in Georgia.
  • Never touch any downed or low-hanging wire. Warn those going out to play in snow or clear debris to watch for and avoid downed wires.
  • Never pull tree limbs off of power lines yourself or enter areas with heavy snow, debris or downed trees as downed power lines may be concealed. Customers should call 911 or Georgia Power immediately if they see a fallen or low-hanging power line.
  • Take care if using a portable generator. Follow all manufacturer's instructions, avoid using generators in enclosed spaces and be mindful of electrical safety. Click here for more safety tips.

Stay Connected & Informed:

  • Outage Alerts – Customers subscribed to our free Georgia Power Outage Alert service will receive personalized notifications and updates via text message. Check that your contact number is up to date to receive the latest information.
  • Social Media – Follow Georgia Power across Facebook, Instagram and X for storm tips, outage updates, customer service and more.
  • Outage & Storm Center – Available at www.GeorgiaPower.com/Storm, customers can visit this site to check their contact information is updated to receive Outage Alerts, report and check the status of outages, and access useful safety tips and information. Customers can also report and check the status of an outage 24 hours a day by contacting Georgia Power at 888-891-0938.
  • Outage Map – Housed within the Outage & Storm Center, Georgia Power's interactive Outage Map provides near real-time information, allowing users to see where outages are occurring across the state and track estimated restoration times. The map is updated regularly from teams in the field.
  • Georgia Power Mobile App – Download the Georgia Power mobile app for Apple and Android devices to access storm and outage information on the go.

About Georgia Power 

Georgia Power is the largest electric subsidiary of Southern Company (NYSE: SO), America's premier energy company. Value, Reliability, Customer Service and Stewardship are the cornerstones of the company's promise to 2.7 million customers in all but four of Georgia's 159 counties. Committed to delivering clean, safe, reliable and affordable energy, Georgia Power maintains a diverse, innovative generation mix that includes nuclear, coal and natural gas, as well as renewables such as solar, hydroelectric and wind. Georgia Power focuses on delivering world-class service to its customers every day and the company is recognized by J.D. Power as an industry leader in customer satisfaction. For more information, visit www.GeorgiaPower.com and connect with the company on Facebook (Facebook.com/GeorgiaPower), X (X.com/GeorgiaPower) and Instagram (Instagram.com/ga_power). 

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/georgia-power-restores-power-to-230-000-customers-following-winter-storm-cora-302348550.html

SOURCE Georgia Power

data.title: Take these steps to set financial goals for your family in 2025 - and reach them
data.description:
By Brian Page

How you and your partner can make saving easier, spending harder, and teamwork second nature

Many couples are setting financially focused New Year's resolutions with hopeful optimism: save more, spend less, and finally get that debt under control. Yet research shows that 23% of them will quit their resolutions by the end of the first week, and 43% will stop by the end of January.

There's a myth that saving money or reducing debt are only challenges for couples barely scraping by, but that's not true. Almost one in three households in the top 10% of income earners owes credit-card debt.

Reaching your financial goals in the coming year isn't about willpower alone - it's about building systems that make saving easier, spending harder, and teamwork second nature.

Research consistently shows that relying on willpower alone is a losing battle. According to the American Psychological Association (APA), willpower is like a muscle - it gets tired with use. Systems, on the other hand, are reliable, automatic, and far less dependent on daily motivation.

For example, automating savings can remove the temptation to spend extra cash sitting in your checking account. My spouse and I started by setting up automatic transfers to our high-yield savings account on each payday.

Pro tip: Establish your savings accounts at a bank or credit union that is different from where you do your primary banking.

A pro tip is to establish your savings accounts at a bank or credit union that is different from where you do your primary banking. Doing so creates natural barriers to spending. When logging into your primary financial institution, you won't see your savings account balance. Even if you do check all of your balances, moving money between financial institutions is not as convenient as it would be if you moved money from one account to another in the same financial institution.

In my household, we created separate savings accounts for different goals: one for vacations, one for home improvements, and another for emergencies. Naming these accounts made them more tangible, and seeing our progress in each was motivating. Here are more tips:

1. Create a budget that reflects your shared goals: One of the biggest mistakes couples make is failing to align their financial priorities. It is widely understood that money is a leading issue married couples argue about. To reduce financial friction, my spouse and I set aside time for regular "money dates."

During these check-ins, we talk about our short- and long-term goals: vacations, retirement, home improvements. Then we budget accordingly, making sure our spending habits reflect those goals.

We also use budgeting apps to track our expenses in real time. These tools not only help us stick to our budget but also provide a clear picture of where our money is going. Having that transparency has eliminated a lot of stress in our financial conversations.

2. Make spending tougher: Let's face it: Spending is incredibly easy in today's world. With one-click purchases, digital wallets, and credit cards, spending money takes almost no effort. To counter the digital ease of purchases, couples can implement a few simple rules:

-- The 48-hour rule: Before making nonessential purchases over a certain dollar amount, wait 48 hours.

-- Separate accounts for discretionary spending: Couples can establish a separate account for monthly "fun money" allowance.

-- Credit-card locks: For larger goals, couples can lock certain credit cards in a banking app to reduce impulse spending.

-- Use cash for certain expenses: Couples can withdraw a set amount of cash each month for categories like dining out or entertainment. When the cash runs out, that's it.

-- Check-ins: Establish a dollar amount and agree that before spending more than that, you'll run it past your partner. You're not necessarily asking for permission; you're staying on the same page.

3. Tackle debt as a team: If debt is part of your financial picture, it's crucial that you and your partner approach it as a team. Creating a clear repayment plan reduces stress and improves follow-through.

Some couples prioritize debts using the "debt snowball" method (paying off smaller debts first for psychological wins) while making minimum payments on larger balances. Other couples might prefer the "debt avalanche" method (paying off debts with the highest interest rates first).

A hybrid approach can also work: Pay the smaller debts first to gain psychological steam and then switch to the avalanche method, which makes more mathematical sense.

A strategy that is particularly useful for couples fighting about certain debts is to be laser-focused on paying those debts off first.

4. Communicate openly and often: Financial success as a couple isn't just about spreadsheets and apps; it's about communication. Regularly discussing financial goals, fears, and victories creates trust and alignment.

My spouse and I set aside time for an in-depth financial review, often doing so with our financial planner. During this time, we celebrate wins, address challenges, and adjust our plan as needed.

5. Celebrate milestones: Financial goals can feel abstract and distant, which makes it easy to lose momentum. That's why it's important to celebrate milestones along the way. Paid off a credit card? Dinner at our favorite restaurant. Hit your annual savings goal? Reward yourself and your partner. Responsible rewards reinforce responsible money habits.

Brian Page founded Modern Husbands, which provides couples and families with ideas for managing money and the home through its Marriage Toolkit, free newsletter and podcast.

More: Couples can manage their money and beat inflation with this relationship routine

Plus: Engaged couples spend a fortune on weddings. They'd be happier investing in their marriage.

-Brian Page

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-11-25 1146ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: 'I can afford to be generous': How much should I give my stepdaughter for her wedding gift? I want to be fair.
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'I don't have any other children'

Dear Quentin,

How much should I give my stepdaughter for her wedding as a gift? I can afford to be generous, but I also want to do the right thing by them.

I am not enabling them with expensive lifestyle choices, and I do not expect much. This has nothing to do with our help with the wedding.

I don't have any other children. My husband and I try to do everything fairly so his children are treated equally.

Stepmother

Related: My girlfriend and I are having a symbolic 'wedding.' She does not want to lose her health benefits - and I don't want to lose my shirt. Is that wise?

Dear Stepmother,

For some people, $500 or $1,000 is a lot of money. For others, they could drop $5,000 or $10,000 or even $50,000 and not give it a second thought. So your question is relative.

One rule of thumb in such a situation is to do a "Freaky Friday" and reverse roles with your stepdaughter. What would you like or need if you were her?

It also depends on how close you are. If her mother is living, and they have a close relationship, you don't want to stand on the mother-of-the-bride's toes.

In that instance, you may wish to avoid giving her jewelry. You could, given that we live in 2024, consult with your husband's first wife, if you're on good or polite terms.

Ultimately, your stepdaughter will have the answer. If she is too modest to tell you what she may want (so you can ascertain the cost), deposit $1,000, $5,000 or $50,000 in her account.

You can always give the amount of cash you can afford and follow up with a thoughtful gift - maybe it's a honeymoon, or simply two delicate china cups with a thoughtful message.

If you try to find the answer online, you will find yourself filling out all sorts of crackpot wedding calculators.

My favorite weddings have been low-key, relaxed and inexpensive: a reception in someone's backyard or in a restaurant for a long afternoon. The same is true for the bride and groom.

My least favorite have been wildly extravagant affairs in windowless five-star hotel ballrooms. They are impersonal and generic. Give a gift that is the equivalent of a backyard wedding.

There are no rules regarding what you should give as the stepmother-of-the-bride. If you try to find the answer online, you will find yourself filling out all sorts of crackpot wedding calculators.

Give them the equivalent of a new sofa, a down payment on a house (if you're swimming in money and you are very close) or a painting that you choose together.

Your presence, love and support will probably be all she wants on the day and in the leadup. Ask her: "How can I help?" And tell her: "Consider me your lady-in-waiting."

No one ever remembers how much someone gave them on their wedding day, especially their family. In my experience, we only ever remember how people made us feel.

Related: 'Marriage sure does make love suck': My fiancée wants a big, expensive wedding - I want to a downpayment for a house

The Moneyist regrets he cannot respond to letters individually.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

'I never thought they would be in this situation': Our daughter and son-in-law spend money as fast as his parents give it to them. Do we butt in?

'We don't have a joint account': My husband has a tenant from hell. He forces me to pay for all his rental-property expenses. Am I being used?

I give my mother's ailing next-door neighbor $500 a month. She agreed to sell me her house, although she'll continue to live there. Is this wise?

Check out The Moneyist's private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-11-25 1123ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Can January really tell the stock market's future? Let us count the ways.
data.description:
By Mark Hulbert

Looking for bullish signs from the 'First five days of January indicator' and the 'January barometer'

The market's near-term movements are largely unpredictable - even over periods as long as a year.

The U.S. stock market's January gyrations so far tell you next to nothing about how stocks are likely to perform for the rest of the year.

I hinted at this conclusion last week, when it became obvious that the so-called Santa Claus rally period - lasting from Christmas through the first two trading days of January- on Wall Street would be a dud. I said that the market's loss over this period was not a cause for concern, since there is no statistically significant correlation between the stock market's performance during this period and equities' direction over the subsequent year.

So, despite Santa's failure to visit Wall Street, the stock market's expected 2025 return was no better or worse than average. But Wall Street loves to tell bullish stories, and an "average" return is not an exciting narrative. So the bulls have simply shifted their attention to other early-January indicators in hopes of finding one that supports their bull-market thesis.

They are now focusing on alleged patterns such as the "first five days of January indicator" and the "January barometer." According to the former, the market's direction over the first five trading days of the year foretells the market's direction for the full year. According to the latter, the market's direction for all of January has this predictive ability.

These other indicators have barely more statistical significance than the Santa Claus rally, as seen in the above chart. The statistics reported in the table are based on the Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA back to its creation in 1896. Notice that only one of these four - the January Barometer - has a track record that is significant at the 95% confidence level statisticians often use when assessing whether a pattern is genuine.

Furthermore, none of the four indicators - including the January barometer - tells us much about the market's subsequent direction. This is revealed by the indicators' low r-squared, which measures the extent to which each can explain or predict the market's direction from February through December. Even the January barometer's r-squared is under 3% - meaning that virtually all of the variation in the stock market's February-through-March performance has nothing to do with how it performed during January.

Consider those years in which the Dow declined during each of the three early-January indicators - the Santa Claus rally period, the first five trading days of January, and the entire month of January. In such years the stock market from February through December rose 73% of the time. That's barely different from the 75% odds of a rising market when the Dow rose over each of these three early-January periods. This is one reason why the r-squared is so low for the composite indicator that is based on a combination of all three early-January indicators.

To put these indicators' low r-squared into context, consider another indicator based on the average household's equity allocation. This is one of the indicators on which I report in my periodic columns on the stock market's valuation. Its r-squared when predicting the stock market's subsequent-year return is 7.1% - almost three times higher than the January Barometer. Unfortunately for the bulls, as I reported in late December, this valuation indicator is forecasting a well-below-average return for the U.S. stock market in the coming years.

To be sure, a 7.1% r-squared is still low. This reflects the unavoidable fact that the market's near-term movements are largely unpredictable - even over periods as long as a year. But even among these myriad indicators that attempt (mostly in vain) to forecast the market's short-term direction, the ones based on early-January gyrations stand out for being especially worthless.

The bottom line? How the stock market does this year will have next to nothing to do with how it is performing now. There are far more important things for you to focus on than these early-January gyrations.

Mark Hulbert is a regular contributor to MarketWatch. His Hulbert Ratings tracks investment newsletters that pay a flat fee to be audited. He can be reached at mark@hulbertratings.com

More: Tired market timers are just what stocks need to rally again

Plus: Why investors clinging to cash could lose money in U.S. debt-ceiling fight

-Mark Hulbert

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-11-25 1125ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Why deporting undocumented workers will stoke inflation and sink Social Security
data.description:
By Peter Morici

Inflation, which appears to be settling at about 2.5% annually, could jump to 4.5%

Screen applicants for their prospective contribution to the U.S. economy, and prioritize those who fill needed occupations.

Economic constraints and public sentiment often require politicians to walk back or moderate campaign promises. For President-elect Donald Trump, proposed policies on mass deportation and immigration would be a good place to start.

President Joe Biden muffed the immigration issue by reversing most of Trump's tough border policies - including requiring many migrants to wait in Mexico while asylum claims could be heard.

Many of these migrants found work and proved critical to sustaining the 2.5% pace of economic growth the U.S. enjoyed through the eight years spanning the first Trump administration and then Biden's term - well above the 1.9% accomplished during the 16 years of the George W. Bush-Barack Obama era.

Following the COVID shutdowns, the U.S. economy rapidly recovered and was at full employment in the summer of 2023. Over the next 14 months, the economy added 187,000 jobs a month. Indigenous population growth and regular immigration alone could have supported only about 80,000 jobs a month.

Irregular immigrants make up this difference. They account for half of agricultural workers in the U.S. and are prominently represented in construction, hospitality and caregiving.

The cost of mass deportation could reach $900 billion.

Vice President-elect JD Vance argues these workers could be replaced by offering Americans higher wages. That's silly.

In a U.S. economy with just 7 million job seekers currently, it's tough to conceive of wages rising enough to motivate more than 1 million Americans to move to California, Iowa and Wisconsin to pick avocados, slaughter cattle, and tend dairy cows - especially when the construction sector simultaneously would need to replace an estimated 1.5 million workers who are undocumented.

The combination of workers deported, fleeing to Canada or going into hiding would create significant food shortages nationwide and stoke the kind of grocery-price inflation suffered during and following the pandemic shutdowns. New home construction would slow precipitously, rents would rise faster, and many women would leave the workplace for want of childcare.

Inflation, which now appears to be settling at about 2.5% annually, could jump to 4.5%. U.S. economic growth would slow dramatically or cease altogether, retirement incomes would be impaired by an anemic U.S. stock market and the depletion of the Social Security funds would accelerate.

The cost of mass deportation could reach $900 billion - enough to build close to 3 million homes or 43,450 elementary schools.

Americans may want better-enforced borders and immigration laws but according to a recent Pew Trust poll, 64% favor letting irregular immigrants who are already here stay, if they meet conditions such as passing a background check.

Otherwise, Americans facing shrinking real incomes, shortages of basic services like childcare, eldercare, home and office cleaners and restaurant help - plus witnessing draconian images of soldiers and sheriffs dragging immigrants from workplaces and homes to prison-like holding facilities - would permit Trump's critics to paint him in the darkest of images.

With Republicans currently holding only a slim majority in the House, prospects for much of Trump's economic and foreign policy priorities would be impaired. In the propaganda competition with China and Russia for influence in emerging nations, America's brand as the champion of human rights would be terribly damaged.

A workable immigration policy

The current system in the U.S. doesn't allow enough regular, legal immigrants to settle, creating worker shortages.

The current system in the U.S. doesn't allow enough regular, legal immigrants to settle, creating worker shortages including in high-tech. The system is overly biased toward family reunification, which can be abused through chain immigration, and a diversity lottery.

Instead, the U.S. should increase quotas to ensure 1 to 1.5 million additional workers a year. As Canada does, screen applicants for their prospective contribution to the U.S. economy, and prioritize those who fill needed occupations.

Let employers sponsor workers but pay fees set by auction and use the proceeds to assist local governments with resettlement costs. Employers should be required to guarantee work for a minimum period of perhaps a year or two, subject to safeguards to prevent churning.

It's not just blue-collar and low-wage occupations that suffer shortages - increasing quotas for engineers and specialized technology workers would likely accelerate U.S. growth.

Raising the cost to employers of immigrant workers through auctioned licenses would greatly reduce employer incentives to hire immigrants and thereby avoid paying higher wages to both native-born Americans and established green card holders.

The U.S. needs more, not fewer, immigrants. Strong, immigration-enabled growth would bring Americans more secure retirements , larger Social Security tax receipts and a more robust stock market.

Peter Morici is an economist and emeritus business professor at the University of Maryland, and a national columnist.

-Peter Morici

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-11-25 1108ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

Baby Shark Universe (BSU), the Web3 gaming project of Pinkfong, has highlighted $BABYSHARK as an exciting development in its ongoing efforts to explore innovative web3 applications for the Baby Shark IP. Beyond music, the brand boasts a strong presence in licensed products, educational content, and media that have delighted audiences worldwide.

With $BABYSHARK, the Baby Shark phenomenon is extending its reach into Web3, merging its massive global fandom with web3's transformative potential.

About $BABYSHARK

$BABYSHARK is the first Baby Shark-inspired community-driven meme coin, dedicated to bringing the global Baby Shark phenomenon into the decentralized world of Web3. The project aims to create a vibrant ecosystem where fans and web3 enthusiasts can celebrate, engage, and innovate together.

data.title: What Happens If I Don’t Pay My Credit Card Bill for Years?
data.description:
NEW YORK CITY, NY / ACCESSWIRE / January 11, 2025 / There could be many reasons why someone might let their credit cards go unpaid for months or even years. This may result from a major financial hardship such as a job loss, divorce, or medical issue. On the other hand, it may also be due to financial negligence if the person thinks there won't be serious consequences.

Below we'll discuss what happens when credit card bills go unpaid for an extended time, and how something like a debt consolidation loan may be able to help you from falling into financial

How long before unpaid credit card debt gets charged off?

Credit card companies aren't going to wait indefinitely to be repaid. At first, when payments are missed, they will typically impose late fees and/or a penalty interest rate that's higher than your standard one. And keep in mind that interest charges will build daily on the unpaid balance due. This will cause the debt to compound and grow larger the longer the situation persists.

Eventually, the credit card company will decide that the cardholder is unlikely to pay their bill in full. This generally occurs between 90 and 180 days after the account becomes delinquent. The credit card company will "charge off" the account when this happens. This means it writes it off as bad debt, which has implications for both its balance sheets and tax purposes. Additionally, the cardholder's account will be closed and no further charges can be made.

Meanwhile, the outstanding debt does not go away. The debt may be sold to a third-party collection agency. The collection agency can then pursue repayment, hoping to profit from the unpaid debt obligations it now owns.

Collections will pursue your debt

Once a collection agency has your account, things can potentially become tense. They might file a lawsuit against you in an attempt to recoup what's owed. This could result in the following:

  • Garnished wages

  • Liens against your property

  • A freeze is placed on your bank accounts

Your credit score will drop

Another thing a collection agency will do is report the account to the three major credit bureaus, Transunion, Equifax, and Experian. This will appear as a major red flag on your credit report and can significantly reduce your credit score.

FICO, a company responsible for calculating credit scores, weighs payment history by as much as 35% of your score. An account being sent to collections will have a significant negative impact on your credit score.

Unfortunately, when this item appears on your credit report, it will typically stay there for seven years whether it's paid or goes unpaid. Unless the collections claim can be disputed on the grounds of an error or inaccuracy, it's unlikely that this item will be removed before then. That could significantly impact your ability to be approved for new loans or credit lines, such as a mortgage, auto loan, or credit cards.

The only way around being sent to collections is to not get into the situation in the first place. If you're experiencing payment issues with credit card or personal loan debt, you may want to consider a debt consolidation loan. This loan can be used to pay off other high-interest, unsecured loans. Debt consolidation loans typically have a lower fixed interest rate, making for a more manageable payment.

The bottom line

When credit card debt goes unpaid for an extended time, it will eventually be sent to collections. This will appear as a negative item on your credit report for the next seven years and significantly cause your credit score to drop. To avoid this, do your best not to let things get to this point by considering your options, such as a debt consolidation loan.

SPONSORED CONTENT

CONTACT:

Sonakshi Murze
Manager
sonakshi.murze@iquanti.com

SOURCE: OneMain Financial

data.title: My 90-year-old parents' neighbor is friendly, nice and, I suspect, a predator. Am I wrong?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'He tells my parents to ignore me when I suggest that it's time to look into senior-living situations'

Dear Quentin,

I truly think that my 90-year-old parents' next-door neighbor is trying to get his hands on their house, and maybe their money. They live in Florida. He is friendly and nice, but he tells lies about me to my parents. He tells my parents to ignore me when I suggest that it's time to look into senior-living situations. He has also convinced them to call him in times of need, and not us, their children. I am the executor of the estate.

How do I protect my parents and the estate from this predator?

Faithful Shepherd

Related: I'm the executor of my 90-year-old aunt's estate. A neighbor threatened me and wants to buy her house. What should I do?

Dear Faithful,

If you are the shepherd, that makes your neighbor the wolf. There is, in fact, one thing wolves have in common with human predators. They like to isolate their intended victim from the pack, their family, community, friends and even their caregivers. It's a hallmark of elder abuse, and usually the first line of attack. Planting the seeds of distrust is also a somewhat base, if effective, way of slowly gaining the confidence of a vulnerable elderly friend, neighbor or family member.

It does sound like this neighbor is laying the groundwork for unwelcome interference in their affairs. Tell your parents about your concerns; that is something to keep an eye on and, if it gets worse, to let you know. Typically, if you suspect someone of elder abuse - emotional, physical, psychological or financial - you should report them to adult protective services, or call 911 and report them to local law-enforcement authorities or your district attorney's office.

As tempting as it is to confront this alleged or future abuser, keep your powder dry. The American Psychological Society recommends against such confrontations as it would likely add fuel to the fire, feed into the abuser's need for drama and division and, as such, could backfire. "Don't put the older adult in a more vulnerable position by confronting the abuser yourself unless you have their permission and are able to help the person experiencing abuse immediately by moving them to a safe place," the APA says. Read more here.

Related: 'I'm guilty of helping too much': My married adult son constantly demands money. How do I put an end to his mooching?

The National Center on Elder Abuse, a government agency affiliated with the U.S. Administration on Aging, says that one in 10 people over the age of 60 in the U.S. experienced some form of abuse in the prior year. Research still lags all the new forms of financial abuse and, as always, prevention is better than the cure. Given your parents' age, this is the time to create a durable and medical power of attorney.

Financial signs of elder abuse include fraudulent signatures on documents, overdue bills and "unusual or sudden changes in spending patterns, will or other financial documents," according to the nonprofit National Council on Aging. It may seem unthinkable, but caretakers, friends and family members are among the most common perpetrators of elder financial abuse. Such crimes cost elderly people up to $28 billion annually.

This is the time to create a durable and medical power of attorney. Put their documents in a safe place, and perhaps add yourself as a co-signer to their accounts.The Moneyist

About those seeds of distrust: "Isolation is a red flag and many studies of elder abuse say a lack of a good support system and physical and psychological isolation are hallmarks of the problem," according to the National Adult Protective Services Association. Your parents have each other, but when one of them passes away, the remaining spouse will be very vulnerable. Put their documents in a safe place, and perhaps add yourself as a co-signer to their accounts.

Ask your parents to keep you updated on any further unwelcome intervention from your parents' dastardly neighbor. Your parents may also be comfortable telling their neighbor: "I appreciate your concern, but we trust our children completely and we don't need any input with our estate planning." Or simply: "Thank you, but I don't wish to discuss our estate planning." Or, if the person becomes increasingly problematic: "I'll pass on your feedback to our children. Now let's change the subject."

Stay alert and be prepared to raise further alarms if the neighbor persists in trying to inveigle their way into your parents' lives.

Related: My wife and I passed on a family wedding due to a 'no-child' rule. We're now attending a friend's nuptials without our kids. Are we hypocrites?

Elder abuse, including neglect and exploitation, is experienced by about 1 in 10 older adults aged 60 and over who live at home, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Such abuse can be physical, sexual, emotional or psychological, financial or negligent.

Anyone who suspects their loved one is experiencing elder abuse can visit the Eldercare Locator website or call 1-800-677-1116 to report concerns. The Eldercare Locator is a public service of the U.S. Administration on Aging.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

'I've never been happier': I married a wonderful lady from Ukraine. I have two successful kids. Do I put my wife on the deed to my home?

'I can afford to be generous': How much should I give my stepdaughter for her wedding gift? I want to be fair.

My sister made frequent withdrawals from the 'bank of Mom and Dad.' Now our family is torn apart. What can I do?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com, and follow Quentin Fottrell on X, the platform formerly known as Twitter.

The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.Check out The Moneyist's private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-11-25 1044ET

data.title: Shop CFP National Championship Merchandise at Rally House
data.description:
LENEXA, Kan., Jan. 11, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Rally House, the fastest growing officially licensed sports apparel and merchandise retailers in the nation, hustled to release their 2025 CFP National Championship Bound collections just seconds after the final whistle blew Thursday night when the Notre Dame Fighting Irish defeated the Penn State Nittany Lions and last night when the Ohio State Buckeyes defeated the Texas Longhorns to punch their tickets to the CFP National Championship Game. Merchandise was available immediately following the games at www.rallyhouse.com, with items arriving in select store locations as quickly as Monday morning. The Rally House website allows shoppers to browse both teams’ entire product offerings and has the function to select a specific Rally House location to see what inventory is available in real time.

Rally House takes great pride in being a part of the fan journey during the most exciting times in sports as teams compete for championships. “You can feel the excitement building from fans shopping our stores for Notre Dame and Ohio State gear” says VP of Marketing Strategy, Aaron Johnson. “Notre Dame and Ohio State have two of the most passionate fans bases in all of sports, and we are constantly bringing in new arrivals and restocking our hottest sellers to get fans the gear they are wanting, when they need it, heading into the CFP National Championship Game,” added Johnson. With a vast selection of Notre Dame and Ohio State merchandise to shop online and in-store at select Rally House locations, Rally House is a must shop spot fans leading up to and after the National Championship Game to gear up. From College Football Playoff t-shirts and hats to sweatshirts and home décor, Rally House truly has something for every Buckeye and Fighting Irish fan.

Rally House encourages customers to browse their website often and visit their local Rally House locations regularly to shop the latest gear. Fans can purchase both Ohio State Buckeyes and Notre Dame Fighting Irish College Football Playoff and Bowl Game gear now online. Customers can expect to have College Football Playoff National Champions merchandise available online for the winning team just seconds after the result of the game has been determined.

About Rally House 
Rally House and Sampler Stores Inc. is a family-owned specialty boutique that offers a large selection of apparel, hats, gifts and home décor representing local NCAA, NFL, MLB, NBA, NHL, and MLS teams in addition to locally inspired apparel, gifts and food. Proudly based in Lenexa, Kansas, Rally House operates 275+ locations across 22 states.

CONTACT:
Aaron Johnson, VP of Marketing Strategy
media@rallyhouse.com

data.title: SHAREHOLDER NOTICE: Halper Sadeh LLC Investigates KAVL, ZUO, SDIG on Behalf of Shareholders
data.description:
NEW YORK, Jan. 11, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Halper Sadeh LLC, an investor rights law firm, is investigating the following companies for potential violations of the federal securities laws and/or breaches of fiduciary duties to shareholders relating to:

Kaival Brands Innovations Group, Inc. (NASDAQ: KAVL)’s merger with Delta Corp Holdings Limited. If you are a Kaival shareholder, click here to learn more about your rights and options.

Zuora, Inc. (NYSE: ZUO)’s sale to Silver Lake and an affiliate of GIC Pte. Ltd. for $10.00 per share in cash. If you are a Zuora shareholder, click here to learn more about your rights and options.

Stronghold Digital Mining, Inc. (NASDAQ: SDIG)’s sale to Bitfarms Ltd. for 2.52 shares of Bitfarms for each share of Stronghold. Upon closing of the proposed transaction, Stronghold shareholders are expected to own just under 10% of the combined company. If you are a Stronghold shareholder, click here to learn more about your legal rights and options.

Halper Sadeh LLC may seek increased consideration for shareholders, additional disclosures and information concerning the proposed transaction, or other relief and benefits on behalf of shareholders. We would handle the action on a contingent fee basis, whereby you would not be responsible for out-of-pocket payment of our legal fees or expenses.

Shareholders are encouraged to contact the firm free of charge to discuss their legal rights and options. Please call Daniel Sadeh or Zachary Halper at (212) 763-0060 or email sadeh@halpersadeh.com or zhalper@halpersadeh.com.

Halper Sadeh LLC represents investors all over the world who have fallen victim to securities fraud and corporate misconduct. Our attorneys have been instrumental in implementing corporate reforms and recovering millions of dollars on behalf of defrauded investors.

Attorney Advertising. Prior results do not guarantee a similar outcome.
